claims.claim_text: A single NVIDIA A100 node costs $12-30/hour on cloud providers.
claims.span_hint: line 32 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: A single NVIDIA A100 node costs $12-30/hour on cloud providers.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A100-class GPU nodes can be expensive on cloud providers, so idle capacity burns money quickly.
claims.rationale: The exact price range is provider-, region-, and date-dependent and the module gives no citable price snapshot.

claims.claim_text: Teams request whole GPUs for jobs that use 15% of capacity.
claims.span_hint: line 32 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: Most ML teams treat GPU scheduling as an afterthought: they request whole GPUs for jobs that use 15% of capacity, leave nodes idle overnight, and never set up proper monitoring.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Many ML teams treat GPU scheduling as an afterthought: they request whole GPUs for lightly utilized jobs, leave nodes idle overnight, and never set up proper monitoring.
claims.rationale: The sentence uses an uncited exact utilization figure that is presented as a general fact.

claims.claim_text: Teams routinely waste $50K-$100K/month on underutilized GPU infrastructure.
claims.span_hint: line 34 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: Teams routinely waste $50K-$100K/month on underutilized GPU infrastructure.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Underutilized GPU infrastructure can waste large amounts of money each month.
claims.rationale: The specific monthly dollar range is uncited and too precise to keep.

claims.claim_text: A named startup reduced GPU spend from $240K/month to $80K/month in three weeks after fixing scheduling and right-sizing issues.
claims.span_hint: line 38 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: > A Series B startup came to us after their cloud bill hit $240K/month. Their ML team had 32 A100 GPUs running 24/7 across three clusters. Average utilization? 11%. Fine-tuning jobs that needed 2 GPUs were requesting 8 "just in case." Inference workloads sat on dedicated A100s when an L4 would have been fine. Nobody had configured time-slicing. Nobody had set up preemption. Within three weeks, we cut their GPU spend to $80K/month--same throughput, same training times--by implementing proper scheduling, right-sizing, and spot instances for fault-tolerant jobs. The lead ML engineer said, "We were basically lighting $160K on fire every month."
claims.suggested_rewrite: > Teams often discover that oversized GPU requests, idle inference nodes, and missing sharing controls are driving far more GPU spend than their actual workload needs.
claims.rationale: This is a highly specific unnamed customer anecdote with exact numbers and a quote, so it cannot be retained without a real source.

claims.claim_text: An H100 costs $30,000-$40,000 to purchase and 8x H100 cloud instances can exceed $98/hour.
claims.anchor_text: - A single NVIDIA H100 GPU costs **$30,000-$40,000** to purchase, and cloud instances with 8 H100s can exceed **$98/hour** ($72K/month if left running)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - H100-class GPUs are expensive to buy, and multi-GPU cloud instances can cost tens of dollars per hour if left running.
claims.rationale: Both the purchase price and hourly cloud price are unstable, vendor-specific figures without a cited snapshot.

claims.claim_text: NVIDIA MIG can split supported A100 and H100-class GPUs into up to seven isolated instances.
claims.rationale: The exact A100/H100 maximum-instance claim is real, but the primary NVIDIA MIG guide is on an off-allowlist domain.

claims.claim_text: MIG turns one $15K GPU into 7 smaller ones.
claims.anchor_text: turning one $15K GPU into 7 smaller ones
claims.suggested_rewrite: allowing one GPU to be partitioned into smaller schedulable slices
claims.rationale: The dollar figure is uncited and the phrasing overstates what MIG is doing operationally.

claims.claim_text: Average GPU utilization in enterprise Kubernetes clusters is under 15%, according to Run.ai's 2025 report.
claims.anchor_text: - The average GPU utilization in enterprise Kubernetes clusters is **under 15%**, according to Run.ai's 2025 GPU Utilization Report--meaning 85% of GPU spend is wasted
claims.suggested_rewrite: - GPU utilization is often much lower than teams expect, which is why measuring real utilization matters before buying more capacity
claims.rationale: I could not verify the exact cited report URL and exact percentage, so the precise statistic should be removed.

claims.claim_text: Google's Borg supported GPU scheduling four years before Kubernetes added device plugin support in v1.10 (2018).
claims.anchor_text: - Google's Borg system (Kubernetes' predecessor) supported GPU scheduling internally **4 years before** Kubernetes added device plugin support in v1.10 (2018)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Google's Borg system handled large-scale internal scheduling before Kubernetes later standardized GPU access through device plugins
claims.rationale: The module combines multiple historical claims and dates that are not directly backed by one clear primary source.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes relies on device plugins to advertise specialized hardware such as GPUs.
claims.span_hint: line 53 | section: GPU Device Plugin Architecture
claims.rationale: The shared device-plugin source directly states that vendors use device plugins to advertise hardware resources to kubelet.

claims.claim_text: GPU resources are integer-only and cannot be shared between containers by default, so requesting `nvidia.com/gpu: 1` gives one whole device.
claims.span_hint: line 85 | section: GPU Device Plugin Architecture
claims.rationale: The shared device-plugin doc explicitly says extended resources are integer-only and devices cannot be shared between containers.

claims.claim_text: The NVIDIA GPU Operator automates management of drivers, the container runtime, the device plugin, and monitoring components.
claims.rationale: The upstream GPU Operator repository README explicitly describes the components it automates.

claims.claim_text: The GPU Operator includes automatic node labelling as part of the managed NVIDIA software stack.
claims.rationale: The upstream GPU Operator README includes automatic node labelling among the managed components.

claims.claim_text: CUDA time-slicing allows multiple workloads to share a GPU but does not provide memory isolation, and all sharers remain in the same fault domain.
claims.span_hint: line 176 | section: GPU Sharing: Time-Slicing and MIG
claims.rationale: The NVIDIA device-plugin repo directly documents the lack of isolation and shared fault domain for time-slicing.

claims.claim_text: MIG provides hardware-level isolation with separate memory and compute resources for each instance.
claims.span_hint: line 214 | section: GPU Sharing: Time-Slicing and MIG
claims.rationale: The primary NVIDIA MIG guide directly covers the isolation semantics, but it is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Spot/preemptible GPU instances cost 60-90% less than on-demand.
claims.span_hint: line 321 | section: Cost Optimization with Spot GPU Instances
claims.anchor_text: Spot/preemptible GPU instances cost 60-90% less than on-demand.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Spot or preemptible instances can be substantially cheaper than on-demand, though the exact discount varies by provider and market.
claims.rationale: The savings principle is real, but the exact cross-provider percentage for GPU instances is too broad to state as a hard fact here.

claims.claim_text: PyTorch Lightning and Hugging Face Trainer have built-in checkpointing.
claims.span_hint: line 351 | section: Cost Optimization with Spot GPU Instances
claims.anchor_text: Most frameworks (PyTorch Lightning, Hugging Face Trainer) have built-in checkpointing--make sure it is enabled.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Use whatever checkpointing support your training stack provides, and make sure it is enabled.
claims.rationale: The named-framework capability claim would require framework-specific docs and is unnecessary to keep the operational lesson.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes 1.35 introduced a CoScheduling feature gate as an alpha API.
claims.span_hint: line 359 | section: Gang Scheduling for Distributed Training
claims.anchor_text: **Gang scheduling** solves this by ensuring all pods in a group are scheduled together or not at all. Kubernetes 1.35 introduced the **CoScheduling** feature gate as an alpha API.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Gang scheduling** solves this by ensuring all pods in a group are scheduled together or not at all.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes 1.35 upstream docs describe alpha gang scheduling through the Workload API and GangScheduling plugin, not a CoScheduling feature gate.

claims.claim_text: Volcano or the Coscheduling plugin provide mature gang scheduling support for production use today.
claims.span_hint: line 403 | section: Gang Scheduling for Distributed Training
claims.anchor_text: For production use today, consider **Volcano** (CNCF sandbox project) or **Coscheduling plugin** for kube-scheduler, which provide mature gang scheduling support.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For production use today, many teams still rely on external schedulers or scheduler plugins when they need gang-style placement.
claims.rationale: The sentence makes a dated maturity judgment across multiple projects without a precise primary source.

claims.claim_text: DCGM Exporter exposes NVIDIA GPU metrics for Prometheus.
claims.span_hint: line 409 | section: GPU Monitoring with DCGM Exporter
claims.rationale: The upstream DCGM Exporter repository states that it exposes GPU metrics for Prometheus.

claims.claim_text: Grafana dashboard 12239 is the official NVIDIA DCGM Exporter dashboard.
claims.span_hint: line 452 | section: GPU Monitoring with DCGM Exporter
claims.rationale: The upstream DCGM Exporter repo points readers to Grafana dashboard 12239 as the official dashboard.

claims.claim_text: AMD has a Kubernetes device plugin for exposing AMD GPUs to a cluster.
claims.rationale: The ROCm upstream repository directly describes the AMD GPU device plugin for Kubernetes.

claims.claim_text: Intel provides a Device Plugins Operator and GPU device plugin for Kubernetes.
claims.rationale: The Intel upstream repository documents both the GPU device plugin and the Device Plugins Operator.

claims.claim_text: NVIDIA has universal ML framework support, AMD has good PyTorch but limited TensorFlow support, and Intel has growing PyTorch/oneAPI support.
claims.anchor_text: | **ML Framework Support** | Universal (PyTorch, TF, JAX) | PyTorch (good), TF (limited) | PyTorch (growing), oneAPI DPC++ |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **ML Framework Support** | Broad support across major ML frameworks | Support varies by framework and version | Support depends on Intel's plugin and oneAPI stack |
claims.rationale: This row makes sweeping cross-vendor compatibility judgments that are too broad and version-sensitive to keep as hard facts.

claims.claim_text: NVIDIA offers time-slicing and MIG, AMD has no equivalent to MIG, and Intel uses SR-IOV-based partitioning.
claims.anchor_text: | **GPU Sharing** | Time-slicing + MIG | No equivalent to MIG | SR-IOV based partitioning |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **GPU Sharing** | Time-slicing and MIG on supported hardware | Sharing options vary by product stack | Sharing options vary by product stack |
claims.rationale: The row compresses nuanced vendor- and product-specific sharing features into hard categorical claims without citations.

claims.claim_text: NVIDIA is on all major clouds, AMD is limited to Azure and some AWS, and Intel Flex/Max is only on select clouds.
claims.anchor_text: | **Cloud Availability** | All major clouds | Limited (Azure, some AWS) | Intel Flex/Max on select clouds |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Cloud Availability** | Widely available | Availability varies by cloud and region | Availability varies by cloud and product line |
claims.rationale: Cloud availability is a fast-moving vendor matrix and the row is too specific to keep without per-cloud sourcing.

claims.claim_text: NVIDIA GPUs cost $10K-$40K, AMD is 30-50% cheaper for similar performance, and Intel is competitive for inference.
claims.anchor_text: | **Cost** | Premium ($10K-$40K/GPU) | 30-50% cheaper for similar perf | Competitive for inference |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Cost** | Premium pricing | Lower-cost options may exist for some workloads | Can be cost-effective for some inference use cases |
claims.rationale: The row mixes exact prices and performance-normalized discount claims that require current benchmark and pricing evidence.

claims.claim_text: AMD MI300X is competitive with H100 for many workloads, but NVIDIA still has a significant tooling and community gap advantage.
claims.anchor_text: **The honest take**: NVIDIA dominates. ROCm has made impressive strides--PyTorch on AMD MI300X is competitive with H100 for many workloads--but the ecosystem gap in tooling, monitoring, and community support is still significant. Choose AMD or Intel only if you have a specific cost or vendor strategy.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **The honest take**: NVIDIA remains the default choice for many ML teams, but AMD and Intel can still be reasonable options when their software support, hardware availability, and cost profile fit your workload.
claims.rationale: The paragraph makes uncited comparative benchmark and ecosystem claims that are too broad to preserve as factual statements.

claims.claim_text: Kueue is Kubernetes-native job queueing and supports fair sharing.
claims.rationale: The upstream Kueue repository explicitly describes Kueue as Kubernetes-native job queueing and lists fair sharing among its features.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes: Schedule GPUs
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the canonical upstream reference for how Kubernetes exposes and schedules GPU resources.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes: Device Plugins
further_reading.why_relevant: This explains the kubelet registration flow, integer extended resources, and why GPU access depends on device plugins.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes: Gang Scheduling
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the upstream description of the v1.35 alpha gang-scheduling model and its all-or-nothing semantics.

further_reading.title: NVIDIA GPU Operator
further_reading.why_relevant: This upstream repository describes the operator-managed NVIDIA GPU stack and is the best allowlisted source for operator scope.

notes: Code blocks, quiz answers, and hands-on exercise steps were intentionally not seeded per schema rules. Several NVIDIA-specific claims are real but would need allowlist expansion because the primary MIG documentation is hosted on docs.nvidia.com, which is currently off allowlist.
